ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fried kuning (yellow banded trevally) fish. Mapped to the broader 'fried fish food product' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Fish, fried) used as the closest defensible category. No species-specific FoodEx2 code exists for yellow banded trevally (Selaroides leptolepis).
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried kuning fish or yellow banded trevally as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried kuning fish is one whole small fish weighing approximately 100 g, commonly served as a side dish with steamed rice in a Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Fried Kuning Fish (F03020016)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Fried Kuning Fish (record ID F03020016). Saturated fat (1.8 g/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried trevally as HPB does not publish fatty acid fractions separately. Potassium (~380 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for fried trevally as HPB does not report potassium for this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index — Not Applicable (negligible carbohydrate content)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fried kuning fish contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (F03020016). Per the University of Sydney GI Database methodology, glycemic index testing is not performed on foods with negligible carbohydrate content as they produce no measurable postprandial glucose response. Glycemic index is therefore not applicable; gi_category set to 'not_applicable'; glycemic_index set to null; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 by definition.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 329 mg/100 g — borderline elevated; partially offset by potassium ~380 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100 g and avoid adding salt or high-sodium dipping sauces, as the 329 mg/100 g sodium content approaches the 400 mg/100 g high-sodium threshold; the estimated potassium content of ~380 mg/100 g provides a meaningful Na:K ratio benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein 25.1 g/100 g supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried kuning fish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it contains 0 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g, producing no postprandial glucose rise; the high protein content (25.1 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic control, though the deep-fried preparation adds fat calories that should be accounted for in total daily energy targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation increases total fat to 8.2 g/100 g; saturated fat estimated ~1.8 g/100 g; sodium 329 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume fried kuning fish in moderation (100 g serving), noting that deep-frying raises total fat content and sodium (329 mg/100 g) is borderline elevated; grilled or steamed preparation is preferred to reduce fat-derived caloric load and minimise advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation associated with cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from trevally provide anti-inflammatory benefit; deep-frying generates AGEs that may worsen joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kuning fish provides omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory joint benefits and calcium (249 mg/100 g) supports bone protection in RA patients; however, deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may exacerbate joint inflammation, so grilled or steamed preparation is strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried kuning fish (Ikan Kuning Goreng) is a popular Singaporean seafood dish made from yellow banded trevally (Selaroides leptolepis), a small, firm-fleshed marine fish with a mild, slightly sweet flavour. The fish is typically seasoned with salt and deep-fried whole in hot oil until the skin is golden-brown and crispy while the flesh remains moist and tender inside. It is a staple in Singaporean hawker centres and home cooking, commonly served as a side dish alongside steamed white rice, sambal, and vegetables. Nutritionally, fried kuning fish is high in protein (25.1 g/100 g) and provides notable calcium (249 mg/100 g) and iron (1.7 mg/100 g), making it a nutrient-dense protein source. Carbohydrate content is zero, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns. Sodium content of 329 mg/100 g is borderline elevated and should be monitored in hypertensive individuals. The deep-frying process raises total fat to 8.2 g/100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. The dish is halal and gluten-free in its basic preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried kuning fish?
Is fried kuning fish suitable for diabetics?
Is fried fish high in sodium?
Can I eat fried fish with hypertension?
Is kuning fish high in protein?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (record ID F03020016), which is the canonical primary source for Singaporean food composition. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried trevally data as HPB does not publish fatty acid fractions; this single field is medium confidence. Overall record confidence is high.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (F03020016); fried kuning fish contains no carbohydrate-contributing ingredients in its basic preparation — confirmed zero. Sugar_g is confirmed zero by the same source — confirmed zero. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as fish contains no dietary fibre — naturally zero. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per University of Sydney GI Database methodology for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Saturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fried trevally as HPB Singapore Food Composition Database does not report fatty acid fractions separately. Potassium_mg (~380 mg/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for fried trevally; not reported in HPB for this entry. Fields set to -1 (data not available in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g — detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractions for fried kuning fish are not available in HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Rheumatoid arthritis health context added from input record as an additional condition.
